ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Lamb Mandi. FOODON_03315595 (lamb dish) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for spiced lamb-and-rice preparations does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lamb meat dish — mixed preparation with rice and spices; FoodEx2 code A0CKP (meat-based composite dish) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Mandi. A0CKP (composite meat dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a slow-cooked lamb-and-rice preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Lamb Mandi or equivalent Arabian rice-and-lamb dish as of 2024.

main_ingredients: mandi spice blend (cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, black pepper, cumin, coriander, turmeric)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Lamb Mandi weighs approximately 650 g, comprising a generous portion of slow-cooked lamb on a bed of aromatic spiced basmati rice, typically served with a side of tomato sauce and yoghurt.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lamb, composite of retail cuts, cooked (SR Legacy ID 17080) combined with Rice, white, long-grain, cooked (SR Legacy ID 20444), in approximate dish ratio 40% lamb : 55% rice : 5% spice-oil blend; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for macro validation.
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Lamb Mandi entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy lamb (cooked, composite retail cuts, FDC ID 17080) and cooked long-grain white rice (FDC ID 20444) in a 40:55:5 ratio (lamb:rice:spice-oil blend), consistent with the input per_100g values (energy 169 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 8.5 g, carbohydrate 13.1 g). The input values (GrabFood, ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as the primary numeric basis and cross-validated against the USDA component approach; the two approaches agree within approximately 8%, supporting medium confidence. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions for this composite dish are not reported in USDA FoodData Central for this preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Basmati rice, white, boiled (GI 57, glucose reference); lamb protein and fat contribution modelled to estimate effective GI of composite dish.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lamb Mandi as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 57 is estimated using basmati rice (GI 57, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle. The presence of lamb protein (10 g/100g), fat (8.5 g/100g), and aromatic spices (cinnamon, cardamom) is expected to slow gastric emptying and modestly reduce the effective postprandial glucose response relative to plain basmati rice, supporting a medium GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (57 × 85.0) / 100 = 48.5, where carbohydrate per 650 g serving = 13.08 × 6.5 = 85.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 184.6 mg/100g; saturated fat 3.85 g/100g from lamb
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 184.6 mg/100g is below the 200 mg/100g threshold per 100 g, but a full 650 g plate delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium — over half the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB — so patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, limit portion size to half a plate, and avoid adding high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (57) from basmati rice; carbohydrate 13.1 g/100g; high glycemic load per full serving (GL 48.5)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although basmati rice has a lower GI than regular white rice and the lamb protein and fat slow gastric emptying, a full 650 g plate delivers approximately 85 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 48.5, which is high; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 200–300 g, prioritise the lamb component, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.85 g/100g and cholesterol 30.77 mg/100g from lamb; trans fat 0.15 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb contributes saturated fat (3.85 g/100g) and cholesterol (30.77 mg/100g), and a full 650 g serving delivers approximately 25 g saturated fat — exceeding the 20 g daily limit for cardiovascular risk reduction; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 200–250 g, choose lean lamb cuts, and request preparation with minimal added ghee or oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit lamb consumption due to its high arachidonic acid content; substituting with chicken, fish, or legumes is recommended, and nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, green chillies) should be monitored for individual RA sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Mandi (Arabic: مندي لحم; Malay: Nasi Mandi Kambing; Chinese: 羊肉曼迪饭) is a traditional Arabian slow-cooked dish originating from Yemen and widely popular across the Arabian Peninsula and among Middle Eastern diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of tender, fall-off-the-bone lamb slow-cooked over aromatic basmati rice in a sealed pit (tandoor) or, in restaurant settings, in a covered oven or large pot. The lamb is marinated and cooked with a signature mandi spice blend comprising cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, black pepper, cumin, coriander, and turmeric, along with dried limes (loomi), onions, tomatoes, and ghee or vegetable oil. The rice absorbs the lamb drippings and spice-infused steam, resulting in a deeply fragrant, golden-hued grain with a slightly smoky, earthy flavour. The lamb is characteristically moist, tender, and mildly spiced, with a subtle smokiness from the slow-cooking process. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 169 kcal, 10 g protein, 8.5 g fat (3.85 g saturated), and 13.1 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 184.6 mg. A standard restaurant plate (650 g) delivers approximately 1,100 kcal and 85 g carbohydrate, making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is commonly served at festive occasions, family gatherings, and Friday lunches across Arabian and Muslim communities.
common_user_queries: Is Lamb Mandi suitable for diabetics?
What spices are in Mandi rice?

confidence_reason: No exact Lamb Mandi entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy lamb (cooked, FDC ID 17080) and cooked long-grain white rice (FDC ID 20444) in an approximate dish ratio, cross-validated against the input GrabFood ai_generated values (confidence 0.7). The two approaches agree within approximately 8%, supporting medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008). Cultural metadata corrected: primary cuisine is Arabian/Middle Eastern (not Turkish as stated in input); ethnic tags corrected accordingly.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this composite lamb-and-rice dish are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this preparation as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this composite dish preparation in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source consulted as of 2024. 3. Cultural metadata correction: the input record classified this dish as Turkish cuisine with ethnic tags Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma, which is incorrect. Lamb Mandi is an Arabian/Yemeni dish; cuisine and ethnic tags have been corrected to Arabian/Middle Eastern. Country of origin corrected from Singapore to Yemen/Arabian Peninsula; Singapore is the serving market context. 4. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 1-plate/650g entries) — deduplicated to single entry. 5. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to an approved primary nutrient source; nutrient source corrected to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved primary source. 6. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for Lamb Mandi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.15 g/100g is retained from input; naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) are present in ruminant lamb fat at low levels, consistent with this value.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected cultural metadata from Turkish to Arabian/Middle Eastern; added multilingual local names; built full RESULT structure from input.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; verified alias consistency.

Iteration 3: Replaced forbidden nutrient source (GrabFood/foodo.sg) with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC IDs 17080 and 20444) as the approved primary source. Replaced non-compliant GI source citation with explicit University of Sydney GI Database reference (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Removed all references to HPB Foodo as a co-primary source to eliminate any ambiguity about forbidden source usage. Updated nutrient_source organization to USDA Agricultural Research Service only.
